test_001.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court ruling on cross-border provisional measures for alleged copyright/trade-secret/unfair-competition misuse would combine ZPO interim-relief rules, international jurisdiction and applicable-law provisions (LugÜ/IPRG), substantive URG/UWG standing and claim norms, plus standard BGG admissibility and leading case law on prima facie proof, urgency, proportionality and limited review under Art. 98 BGG.

test_002.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court judgment on prescription of supplementary road-traffic damages and exoneration for gross negligence would cite the SVG liability/prescription provisions, OR damage rules, standard civil-appeal BGG provisions, basic constitutional review norms, and leading case law on knowledge of damage, later aggravation, and gross negligence of the injured party.

test_003.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on this fact pattern would likely focus on remission of debt under OR, interpretation/acceptance principles, the inapplicability of the VKG to business-purpose leasing, lease-return/refurbishment rules including the debated reach of Art. 267a OR by analogy, plus standard civil-appeal BGG provisions and leading contract/lease jurisprudence.

test_004.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court ruling on timeliness of a bankruptcy petition under Art. 166 Abs. 2 SchKG and annulment of bankruptcy for alleged solvency would cite the SchKG provisions on bankruptcy requisition and appeal, ZPO rules on summary proceedings and notification of decisions, standard BGG admissibility/review/cost provisions, and leading jurisprudence on the 15-month peremptory period, the end of suspension during Rechtsöffnung, and the strict requirements for making solvency probable under Art. 174 Abs. 2 SchKG.

test_005.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on bearer mortgage certificates, novation versus fiduciary transfer, and enforcement by realization would center on the ZGB rules on Schuldbrief, OR rules on novation and contract interpretation, SchKG provisions on pledge-realization and provisional release, plus standard BGG admissibility/cost provisions and the leading BGE on paper mortgage certificates and fiduciary security.

test_006.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on PrHG defect proof and exoneration would cite the core product-liability provisions (defect, exoneration, limitation/damage scope), burden-of-proof rules, possible constitutional complaints on evidence/arbitrariness, and the standard civil-appeal admissibility, fact-review, nova, costs and legal-aid provisions, together with leading case law on proof of defect, appreciation of expert evidence and burden of proof.

test_007.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on medical liability for ophthalmic treatment would center on mandate law (OR 394 ff.), the physician’s contractual duty of care and liability (OR 398, 97), burden and proof of damage, possible unjust enrichment/refund issues, contrast with work-contract provisions, and the standard civil-appeal and costs provisions under the BGG, together with leading BGE on medical treatment as Auftrag and physician liability.

test_008.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on cross-border provisional child maintenance after an alleged wrongful removal would likely combine BGG admissibility rules, IPRG gateway provisions, HKsÜ/HKÜ child-protection and abduction jurisdiction rules, LugÜ maintenance/provisional-measures provisions, and leading BGE on habitual residence, lis pendens, and protective measures.

law_citations: Art. 72 Abs. 2 lit. b Ziff. 1 BGG
test_009.reasoning: The decision would primarily turn on recognition and exequatur of foreign probate and status-related acts under the IPRG, the restrictive ordre-public exception, proof/authentication of foreign public documents, and the bank’s duty/confidentiality vis-à-vis the estate representative, plus standard BGG admissibility and costs provisions.

test_010.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court judgment on co-perpetration of qualified robbery and appellate fact review under Art. 398 StPO would cite the robbery/theft and participation provisions, sentencing rules, in dubio pro reo guarantees, standard BGG admissibility and review norms, plus leading jurisprudence on Mittäterschaft, brigandage, arbitrariness and appellate free review of facts.

test_011.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on this fact pattern would likely address international/local jurisdiction, prorogation and its effect on an assignee, the distinction between Aurora’s own claim and NOVA’s assigned contractual claim, and the substantive basis of liability for unauthorized agency under OR, together with standard BGG admissibility and costs provisions.

test_012.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on this dispute would mainly cite OR provisions on contract interpretation, mandate/accounting, loan, acknowledgement of debt and interruption/new prescription, plus standard civil-appeal BGG provisions and leading case law on mixed contracts, burden of proof/account rendering, prescription and debt acknowledgement.

test_013.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on temporary agency workers’ wage claims under a generally binding CCT would likely combine OR rules on CCT effect and contract interpretation, AVG/AVV provisions on staff leasing and equal-pay/customary-wage references, AVEG rules on extension of CCTs, plus standard civil-appeal BGG citations and leading case law on CCT applicability, interpretation and customary wages.

test_014.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on this UVG/ATSG shoulder-injury dispute would cite the substantive definitions of accident and accident-like lesion, the standard BGG admissibility and review provisions for a public-law appeal in social insurance matters, and leading case law on the elements of an accident, the evidentiary value of initial statements, and the distinction between traumatic and degenerative shoulder pathology.

test_015.reasoning: The decision would likely combine divorce-maintenance law, separation-of-property and interspousal patrimonial claims, qualification of the transfer as mandate versus simple partnership, accounting/restitution duties, burden of proof, interest, and standard civil-appeal procedural citations.

test_016.reasoning: This is a civil appeal on protective measures between spouses concerning interim maintenance, requiring citations on Art. 176 ZGB, summary-proceeding/protective-measures procedure, restricted constitutional review under Art. 98 BGG, and the leading maintenance jurisprudence on self-employed income, incompressible charges, and hypothetical income.

test_017.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on lease termination for arrears and summary eviction would cite the OR rules on default termination, the ZPO rules on cas clairs and appeal/nova, plus standard BGG admissibility/cost provisions and leading case law on receipt of uncollected registered notices, strict validity of Art. 257d OR terminations, and restrictive admission of nova under Art. 317 ZPO.

law_citations: Art. 72 Abs. 2 lit. b Ziff. 1 BGG
test_018.reasoning: The issue is a civil international judicial assistance production order against a non-party, so a Federal Supreme Court decision would likely combine IRSG/IRSV provisions on execution and remedies, ZPO rules on document production and refusal based on trade secrecy, constitutional good-faith/right-to-be-heard guarantees, plus BGG admissibility/costs and leading case law on immediate review of evidentiary orders, defective legal-remedy notices, and refusal rights of third parties.

test_019.reasoning: The case is a private international family-law appeal on Swiss provisional protective measures during foreign divorce proceedings and on recognition/public-policy review of a Moroccan divorce, so a Federal Supreme Court judgment would cite IPRG provisions on jurisdiction, lis pendens, recognition and ordre public, core ZGB/ZPO maintenance-measure rules, standard BGG admissibility/cost provisions, and leading BGE on foreign family judgments and provisional measures.

test_020.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on definitive registration of a Bauhandwerkerpfandrecht with disputed Werklohn, extras, delay penalties and refusal of expert evidence would typically cite the statutory mortgage provisions of the ZGB, core Werkvertrag rules of the OR, proof/right-to-be-heard rules, and standard civil-appeal provisions plus leading Bauhandwerkerpfandrecht and evidentiary jurisprudence.

test_021.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on a freight-forwarding loss would center on the qualification of the Speditionsvertrag between forwarding law and mandate, the distinction between auxiliary liability and sub-mandate under Arts. 398/399 OR, plus standard civil-appeal admissibility, fact review, and costs provisions.

test_022.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on international jurisdiction for marital and child-protection measures in the presence of a foreign divorce action would cite the IPRG provisions on divorce jurisdiction/recognition and provisional measures, the Hague 1996 child-protection jurisdiction rules (habitual residence and perpetuatio fori), the ZGB/ZPO bases for protective and child measures, plus standard BGG admissibility and costs provisions and leading BGE on Art. 10/46 IPRG and international child jurisdiction.

test_023.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court judgment on former-director liability under Art. 52 AHVG would cite the AHVG/AHVV basis of employer contribution duties and damage liability, ATSG/BGG procedural norms, constitutional hearing guarantees, company-law rules on organ status and joint liability, plus the leading BGE on Art. 52 AHVG, differentiated solidarity, causation, fault, and evidentiary/hearing issues.

test_024.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court divorce decision on disputed earning capacity, medical incapacity, evidentiary duties, and the inadmissibility of new appellate requests would likely cite the ordinary-proof and evidence provisions of the ZPO, the divorce/maintenance and matrimonial property rules of the ZGB, constitutional fair-hearing/arbitrariness guarantees, plus the standard civil-appeal BGG provisions and leading case law distinguishing summary protective measures from ordinary divorce proceedings.

test_025.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on this divorce/matrimonial property dispute would likely cite the core ZGB provisions on Errungenschaftsbeteiligung, IPRG rules on foreign immovables and divorce-related jurisdiction/effects, standard BGG admissibility and costs provisions, plus leading BGE on liquidation of co-ownership, classification presumptions under matrimonial property law, burden of proof, and fact review.

test_026.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court divorce/maintenance judgment on these facts would likely combine citations on post-divorce spousal maintenance, child maintenance calculation and modification, extraordinary child costs, and co-ownership/matrimonial property treatment of rental income from a jointly owned asset, plus standard civil-appeal procedural provisions.

law_citations: Art. 72 Abs. 2 lit. b Ziff. 6 BGG
test_027.reasoning: The case concerns child-protection provisional measures, transfer of Obhut/custody, supervised contact and the child’s right to be heard in summary review; a Federal Supreme Court decision would therefore cite the ZGB child-protection framework, investigatory/hearing rules, constitutional and Convention guarantees, the limited-review rules for provisional measures under the BGG, and leading jurisprudence on Kindesanhörung, Kindeswohl and arbitrariness.

test_028.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court judgment on owner’s liability for injuries caused by a building entrance door would center on Art. 58 OR (construction defect/maintenance), causation, burden of proof, contributory fault, and the standard civil-appeal provisions under the BGG, together with the leading BGE on Werkeigentümerhaftung and more recent 4A case law.

law_citations: Art. 72 Abs. 2 lit. b Ziff. 6 BGG
test_029.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on admissibility of an immediate appeal against an expert order in adult-protection proceedings and on the lawfulness/proportionality of a provisional Vertretungs- und Vermögensverwaltungsbeistandschaft would typically cite the BGG rules on appealability of interim measures, the ZGB provisions on investigation and superprovisional/provisional measures, the substantive adult-protection norms (subsidiarity, necessity, proportionality, representation and asset management), plus leading BGE and 5A case law on non-final decisions, difficult-to-repair harm, and Beistandschaft requirements.

test_030.reasoning: This is a matrimonial-protection maintenance dispute under summary proceedings, so a Federal Supreme Court judgment would typically cite the ZGB maintenance/protective-measures provisions, summary-procedure and evidentiary rules, the restricted BGG review framework for provisional measures, arbitrariness/hearing guarantees, and the leading jurisprudence on hypothetical income, adaptation periods, and the two-step surplus method.

test_031.reasoning: A BGer divorce-maintenance judgment on post-marital support after a long marriage would center on Art. 125 ZGB, contrast the lebensprägende Ehe / standard-of-living line with the minimum-subsistence-plus-surplus method, and include the usual civil-appeal admissibility, fact-review, and costs provisions under the BGG.

test_032.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court detention decision on flight risk and substitute measures would cite the constitutional and Convention guarantees of liberty, the StPO provisions on pretrial detention and replacement measures, standard BGG admissibility/cost rules, the underlying fraud/forgery offences, and the leading BGE/1B jurisprudence on serious suspicion, Fluchtgefahr, necessity and proportionality.

test_033.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court UVG occupational-disease judgment here would center on Art. 9 UVG, causation and proof standards, the insurer’s duty to investigate under ATSG, the evidentiary value/necessity of expert reports, and the standard social-insurance appeal provisions of the BGG.

test_034.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on the timeliness of a Bauhandwerkerpfandrecht under Art. 839 ZGB would cite the core lien provisions, ordinary civil-appeal rules under the BGG, and the leading jurisprudence defining completion of work, supplementary works, and the start of the four-month registration period.

test_035.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on whether foreign pending merits proceedings satisfy Art. 263 ZPO in an international succession context would likely cite the core provisions on provisional measures, international succession jurisdiction and recognition, plus standard civil-appeal and constitutional review norms, together with leading ZPO/IPRG case law on superprovisorische measures, foreign main proceedings, and restrictive review under Art. 98 BGG.

test_036.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court ruling on juvenile-ordered DNA profiling would likely cite the JStPO competence rules, StPO provisions on coercive measures/DNA profiling and file access, constitutional proportionality and hearing guarantees, DNA profile legislation, and the standard BGG admissibility/cost provisions together with leading DNA/right-to-be-heard case law.

test_037.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on Swiss trademark/domain-name conflict would center on MSchG exclusivity and infringement remedies, UWG confusion/parasitism, trade-name/name protection by analogy, and standard civil-appeal provisions under the BGG, together with leading BGE on distinctiveness, likelihood of confusion, domain names as signs, and transfer/injunction remedies.

test_038.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court judgment on civil liability for bodily injury, loss of earnings, causation, proof and moral damages would combine OR tort provisions, ZGB/ZPO burden-of-proof and evidence rules, full BGG admissibility/cost provisions, constitutional arbitrariness/hearing guarantees, and leading BGE on causation, proof of damage and Genugtuung assessment.

test_039.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on this dispute would center on contractual qualification under Art. 18 OR and Art. 530 ff. OR, burden of proof under Art. 8 ZGB, invalidity for mistake/fraud under Art. 23, 24, 28, 31 OR, plus standard civil-appeal BGG provisions and leading jurisprudence on simple partnership, contract interpretation, factual proof, and burden allocation.

test_040.reasoning: A Federal Supreme Court decision on abusive invocation of spousal maintenance in measures protecting the marital union would likely cite the core ZGB provisions on marital support and abuse of rights, ZPO rules on Eheschutz and summary proceedings, BGG admissibility and limited-review provisions, plus leading case law on Art. 176 ZGB, Art. 2 Abs. 2 ZGB, and constitutional arbitrariness review.
